steps.title: Read for the passage's dominant emphasis
steps.content: The entire passage hammers one idea: everything in Coketown is the same. Streets are "all very like one another," residents are "equally like one another," they share "the same hours," "the same sound," "the same work," and "every day was the same." The repetition of sameness language is the passage's defining feature — not opportunity, not time, not pleasantness.

steps.title: Eliminate each wrong choice, then confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘To emphasize the uniformity of both the town and the people who live there’: The relentless repetition of "same" and "like one another" applies to streets and residents alike — uniformity is exactly the point. ✓
  • Choice B — ‘To explain the limited work opportunities available to the town's residents’: The passage mentions they all "do the same work" but never explains what that work is or why options are limited — this is one detail, not the main purpose. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘To reveal how the predictability of the town makes it easy for people lose track of time’: The passage says every day resembles the next, but never says anyone loses track of time — that interpretation is unsupported. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘To argue that the simplicity of life in the town makes it a pleasant place to live’: The passage never calls Coketown pleasant or argues it is a desirable place — it reads more as bleak description. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
